# Methods

This systematic review was conducted in accordance with the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA 2020) guidelines [@page2021]. The protocol was designed to capture peer-reviewed evidence on large language model (LLM) applications across the full spectrum of clinical research activity, from single-prompt query systems to autonomous agentic coding interfaces.

## Search Strategy

A comprehensive literature search was performed across three major biomedical databases—Scopus, PubMed (MEDLINE), and Embase—covering publications from January 2024 through March 2026. This date range was selected to capture the period of rapid clinical translation following the widespread availability of GPT-4 and successor foundation models.

Search terms were constructed from three conceptual domains and combined using Boolean operators. The technology domain included: "large language model", "LLM", "ChatGPT", "GPT-4", "generative AI", "foundation model", and "prompt engineering". The clinical application domain included: "agentic", "retrieval-augmented generation", "RAG", "electronic health record", "EHR", and "clinical decision". The research context domain included: "clinical research", "clinical trial", and "medical research". Within each domain, terms were joined with OR; domains were then combined with AND. Truncation and MeSH headings were applied in PubMed; Emtree headings were used in Embase where available. No language or document-type filters were applied at the initial search stage, permitting downstream manual exclusion decisions.

The full Boolean string applied to all databases was structured as follows:

```
("large language model" OR "LLM" OR "ChatGPT" OR "GPT-4" OR
 "generative AI" OR "foundation model" OR "prompt engineering")
AND
("agentic" OR "RAG" OR "retrieval-augmented generation" OR
 "EHR" OR "electronic health record" OR "clinical decision")
AND
("clinical research" OR "clinical trial" OR "medical research")
```

Searches were executed on 15 March 2026 and results were exported in RIS format for deduplication and screening.

## Study Selection and PRISMA Flow

Records were imported into a structured screening pipeline. Deduplication was performed by matching on DOI, title similarity (Jaccard threshold ≥ 0.85), and PubMed ID where available.

The PRISMA 2020 selection process is summarised in @tbl-prisma below.

| Stage | Records |
|:------|--------:|
| Records identified (Scopus: 640; PubMed: 557; Embase: 420) | 1,617 |
| After duplicate removal | 1,063 |
| After title/abstract quality screening | 1,054 |
| After DOI validation via doi.org handle API | 1,027 |
| Full-text articles assessed for eligibility | 127 |
| **Articles included in final synthesis** | **55** |
| Unique journals represented | 47 |

: PRISMA 2020 record flow for the systematic review of LLM applications in clinical research. {#tbl-prisma}

Two reviewers independently screened titles and abstracts; disagreements were resolved by consensus or adjudication by a third reviewer. Full-text retrieval was attempted for all records passing abstract screening. At the full-text stage, 72 articles were excluded for the following reasons: CiteScore below 3.0 (n = 28), editorials or letters (n = 19), non-clinical AI applications (n = 14), unresolvable DOI (n = 7), and preprints without peer review (n = 4). This yielded 55 articles from 47 journals for inclusion in the final synthesis.

## Inclusion Criteria

Articles were included if they met all of the following criteria: (1) peer-reviewed original research, systematic review, or scoping review published in English; (2) publication date within the January 2024 to March 2026 search window; (3) primary focus on the application of an LLM or generative AI system within a clinical research context, defined as studies involving patient data, clinical trials, healthcare decision support, or medical knowledge synthesis; and (4) possession of a validated digital object identifier confirmed accessible via the doi.org resolution API at the time of screening. Studies reporting any quantitative or qualitative outcome measure of LLM performance—including accuracy, clinician agreement, task completion rate, or qualitative usability assessment—were eligible regardless of study design.

## Exclusion Criteria

Records were excluded if they met any of the following criteria: (1) editorials, letters to the editor, conference abstracts without associated full-text manuscripts, or commentary pieces lacking primary data; (2) absent or unresolvable DOI, indicating inability to confirm bibliographic integrity or retrieve the full text; (3) AI or natural language processing applications situated entirely within a computer science or engineering context without a substantive clinical or medical research component; or (4) preprints deposited on servers such as medRxiv or bioRxiv that had not undergone formal peer review at the time of database export. Studies evaluating LLMs exclusively for administrative or billing tasks without clinical research application were also excluded.

## Quality Assessment

Journal-level quality was evaluated using two complementary metrics. CiteScore (Elsevier, 2023 edition) was retrieved for each source journal; only journals with a CiteScore of 3.0 or above—broadly corresponding to Q1 or Q2 quartile standing in their respective subject categories—were retained. For journals not indexed in the Scopus CiteScore database, SCImago Journal Rank (SJR) quartile assignment served as a secondary criterion. This dual-metric approach ensured that clinically oriented journals indexed primarily in Embase or MEDLINE were not systematically excluded due to incomplete Scopus coverage.

Open-access status and full-text URL availability were verified via the Unpaywall API using a registered institutional email address. DOI resolution was performed against the doi.org handle API; any record returning a non-200 HTTP status or redirecting to a retraction notice was excluded at the validation stage. Together, these automated checks reduced the risk of including retracted, misattributed, or inaccessible literature.

The certainty of evidence across each thematic domain was assessed using an adapted GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, Development, and Evaluations) approach, evaluating risk of bias, inconsistency across studies, indirectness, imprecision, and publication bias. Given the predominance of non-randomised benchmarking designs, the overall certainty for most domains was rated as low to moderate.

Study-level methodological quality was appraised using design-appropriate instruments. Diagnostic accuracy studies were evaluated with the QUADAS-2 framework; observational studies with the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale; and AI benchmarking studies using a four-domain checklist adapted from DECIDE-AI, covering task specification clarity, reference standard validity, performance metric appropriateness, and generalisability of the test set. Each article was independently appraised by two reviewers; discrepancies were resolved by discussion.

## Data Extraction and Synthesis

Structured data extraction was performed for all 55 included articles using a pre-specified extraction form capturing: study design, clinical domain, LLM architecture or model name (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2, Med-PaLM 2), primary outcome metric (accuracy, F1-score, AUROC, or clinician agreement rate), sample size or benchmark dataset size, and geographic setting. Extracted fields were double-coded by two independent reviewers; inter-rater reliability was assessed using Cohen's kappa, with discrepancies resolved through adjudication.

Articles were classified into 12 thematic categories reflecting the operational complexity of LLM deployment: (1) diagnostic reasoning assistance, (2) clinical note generation and summarisation, (3) EHR query and phenotyping, (4) clinical trial protocol drafting, (5) patient-facing conversational agents, (6) drug interaction and pharmacovigilance screening, (7) radiology and pathology report interpretation, (8) literature synthesis and evidence retrieval via RAG, (9) genomic variant interpretation, (10) clinical coding and billing automation, (11) surgical and procedural guidance, and (12) agentic multi-step research orchestration. This taxonomy was developed iteratively during pilot extraction and reflects a gradient of increasing autonomy and systemic integration, from discrete prompt-based tools to fully autonomous agentic pipelines capable of iterative reasoning and tool use.

Prior to synthesis, extracted performance metrics were standardised to common scales where possible: accuracy and F1-scores were converted to percentages, and AUROC values were retained as decimal fractions. Outcomes were tabulated by thematic category and displayed using forest-plot-style summary tables for domains with three or more comparable studies. The PRISMA flow diagram (see @tbl-prisma) provides a complete record of the study selection process with exclusion reasons at each stage.

The potential for reporting bias was assessed at the outcome level. Because LLM benchmarking studies are more likely to be published when models achieve favourable results, a positive-outcome publication bias was anticipated. This was considered when interpreting the magnitude of reported performance metrics, particularly for zero-shot evaluations where negative results are less frequently published.

Given the heterogeneity of study designs, LLM architectures, and outcome definitions, a formal meta-analytic pooling of performance metrics was not performed. Instead, findings are presented as a narrative synthesis organised by application complexity. Quantitative performance metrics are reported descriptively, with medians and ranges provided where sufficient homogeneity existed across studies within a thematic category. Convergent findings, areas of consensus, and remaining knowledge gaps are identified for each domain.
